common_name: George L. Taber Azalea (George Tabor Azalea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade; George L. Taber is a large-growing Southern Indian Hybrid azalea requiring excellent drainage and soil acidity — amend clay soils with pine bark and sulfur before planting.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade (morning sun, afternoon shade is ideal); George L. Taber is the quintessential Gulf Coast landscape azalea — a large-growing Southern Indian Hybrid producing enormous quantities of large (3–3.5-inch), pale orchid-pink to light lavender-pink flowers with a distinctive deeper purple-pink blotch on the upper petals in spring. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens it is among the most recognizable and widely planted spring-blooming shrubs, capable of reaching 8–10 feet tall and wide after decades in the landscape. The semi-evergreen foliage is medium-green and holds through zone 9 winters. George L. Taber was selected from open-pollinated seedlings in a Florida nursery and named after the Florida State nurseryman; it became one of the dominant landscape azaleas of the 20th-century South. It blooms mid-season with a spectacular display that lasts 2–3 weeks and is best appreciated when mass-planted or used as a large accent. Requires good drainage — plants in poor drainage develop root rot rapidly. Fertilize after bloom with an acid-forming fertilizer.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Formosa grows very large and should be given ample space from structures and other plants — a mature specimen in zone 9 can reach 12–15 feet in all directions.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Formosa is one of the oldest and most vigorous Southern Indian Hybrid azaleas, a bold, large-growing shrub producing masses of large, vivid purple-magenta flowers — among the most intensely colored of all the common landscape azaleas — in a spectacular mid-spring display. In zone 9 Gulf Coast conditions where winter cold is limited, Formosa can reach remarkable dimensions: 12–15 feet tall and equally wide after decades in the landscape, forming massive, billowing specimen plants that stop traffic during bloom season. The bold magenta-purple color is unmistakable and highly visible from a distance, making it an outstanding landscape accent. Formosa has been grown in the American South since the early 20th century and anchors many older gardens across the Gulf Coast, often outliving the buildings they were planted near. It is semi-evergreen in zone 9, holding most of its large, glossy, dark-green leaves through mild winters. The vivid flower color is too bold to mass indiscriminately — use as a large-scale focal point or planted singly against an evergreen backdrop. Prune immediately after bloom; do NOT prune in fall or winter, which removes flower buds.

common_name: Encore Autumn Monarch Azalea (Reblooming Lavender-Pink Azalea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade (4–6 hours of sun is ideal for rebloom — more sun than traditional azaleas require); Autumn Monarch is one of the larger-growing Encore varieties and benefits from 4–6 hours of direct morning sun to produce the best fall bloom.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade with at least 4 hours of direct sun; Encore Autumn Monarch is one of the leading cultivars in the revolutionary Encore Azalea series — a breakthrough group of reblooming landscape azaleas that bloom in spring AND again in summer and fall, dramatically extending azalea interest beyond the traditional single spring flush. Autumn Monarch produces large (2.5–3-inch), semi-double, lavender-pink flowers in a spring bloom followed by repeated summer and fall flushes that persist until frost. The Encore series was developed by plant breeder Robert E. Lee (Plant Development Services) over 15 years by crossing traditional azaleas with the naturally reblooming Rhododendron oldhamii from Taiwan. Encore varieties require more sun than traditional azaleas — 4–6 hours of direct sun daily maximizes fall rebloom; in deep shade the spring bloom may be adequate but fall rebloom is sparse. Autumn Monarch is one of the larger Encore cultivars, reaching 3.5–5 feet. Fertilize immediately after spring bloom with an azalea-specific acidic fertilizer to support fall rebloom.

common_name: Encore Autumn Fire Azalea (Reblooming Red Azalea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade with at least 4 hours of direct sun; Autumn Fire is a compact Encore variety well-suited to smaller spaces, foundation plantings, and containers.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade with 4–6 hours of direct sun for best rebloom; Encore Autumn Fire is a compact reblooming azalea producing vivid, clear red single flowers in spring and again in summer and fall — one of the few dependably red reblooming azaleas available, as red pigmentation in azaleas can be difficult to achieve with good rebloom habit. The flowers are produced prolifically in spring and recur reliably in summer and fall flushes, making Autumn Fire a standout accent plant for three-season color. The compact habit (24–36 inches tall and wide) fits it well for foundation plantings, borders, and mixed shrub beds where the larger-growing traditional azaleas would overgrow the space. Like all Encore azaleas, it requires more sun than traditional azaleas for reliable fall rebloom — plant where it receives at least half a day of direct sun. The compact size also makes it suitable for large containers. In zone 9 the semi-evergreen foliage typically holds well through winter. Fertilize with an acid-based fertilizer after the spring bloom flush to support subsequent rebloom cycles.

common_name: Encore Autumn Royalty Azalea (Reblooming Purple Azalea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade with 4–6 hours of direct sun; Autumn Royalty is widely considered one of the best-performing Encore azaleas for rebloom quantity and flower color intensity.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade with 4–6 hours of direct sun; Encore Autumn Royalty is one of the most popular and widely recommended Encore azalea cultivars — a medium-sized reblooming shrub producing large (2.5–3-inch), bright purple to violet-purple single flowers in spring with reliable and abundant rebloom through summer and fall. The purple-violet flower color is distinctive and unusual among commonly available landscape azaleas, which tend toward pink, red, and white; Autumn Royalty fills an important color range in the landscape palette. It is widely regarded as one of the strongest reblooming Encore varieties, producing substantial fall flushes when given adequate sun. The medium, upright-rounded habit reaches 3–4 feet and fits foundation plantings and mixed borders without overwhelming adjacent plants. Like all Encore azaleas, performance in fall bloom is directly linked to sun exposure — plants receiving less than 4 hours of direct sun may bloom adequately in spring but produce sparse fall color. Amend planting area with pine bark and acidifying fertilizer to maintain the required pH 4.5–6.0 range, especially in alkaline Gulf Coast soils.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Fashion is a Glenn Dale Hybrid that tolerates more cold than Southern Indian Hybrids — hardy to zone 6 — while still performing excellently in zone 9 heat and humidity.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Fashion is one of the classic Glenn Dale Hybrid azaleas — a group developed by USDA plant breeder Benjamin Yeo Morrison at the Glenn Dale Plant Introduction Station in Maryland from the 1930s through the 1950s to extend azalea hardiness beyond the frost-tender Southern Indian Hybrids while maintaining large flowers and good landscape habit. Fashion produces distinctive, hose-in-hose (flower-within-a-flower) double flowers in a warm salmon-coral to orange-salmon tone — a color combination unusual among landscape azaleas, where pink, purple, and red predominate. The hose-in-hose flower form gives each bloom a doubled, full appearance that is particularly attractive and distinctive. Plants grow vigorously to 4–6 feet in zone 9, with a broad, mounded to spreading habit and semi-evergreen to evergreen foliage. Fashion blooms in mid-spring and is a single-season bloomer (unlike the Encore rebloomers), but the distinctive flower color and form make it a valued addition to the azalea collection. The Glenn Dale Hybrids as a group are underused in modern landscapes compared to the heavily marketed Encore series; Fashion represents a classic selection worth preserving.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — Glenn Dale Hybrid Documentation
